mechanism_narrative: Sustained growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) over the prior 3 months reflects broad economic momentum, leading to higher expected corporate earnings and positive equity returns. This is a persistent, always-on signal of underlying economic strength.
evidence_refs: fact:Consistent job growth is a hallmark of economic expansions.
failure_condition: The signal's walk-forward Information Coefficient (IC) remains below 0.02 for a rolling 3-year period, indicating no predictive power.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp decline in housing starts (HOUST) below the 1.6 million unit annualized rate signals a severe contraction in a key cyclical sector, presaging broader economic weakness and negative returns for the S&P 500 over the next quarter.
evidence_refs: fact:Housing starts have fallen from over 2200 in early 2006 to near 1500 recently.
failure_condition: When HOUST is below 1600, the average 60-day forward S&P 500 return is not statistically different from periods when HOUST is above 1600 (p-value > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: The combination of slowing industrial production (INDPRO) and rising inflation (CPIAUCSL) indicates stagflationary pressure. This dynamic erodes corporate profit margins and increases discount rates, leading to negative equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Stagflationary periods like the 1970s were poor for equity performance.
failure_condition: In periods where the trigger condition is met, the median 20-day forward return for the S&P 500 is positive.

mechanism_narrative: When the Federal Funds Rate (FEDFUNDS) is above 5%, monetary policy is restrictive, increasing the cost of capital for firms and making bonds a more attractive alternative to equities. This valuation pressure leads to negative S&P 500 returns over the medium term.
evidence_refs: fact:The Fed has raised rates from 1% in 2004 to 5.25% currently.
failure_condition: The hit rate (percentage of correct directional predictions) of the signal over a 5-year walk-forward test is less than 55%.

mechanism_narrative: An unemployment rate (UNRATE) below 4.7% indicates a tight labor market nearing full employment. This strength supports robust consumer spending and corporate revenues, driving positive short-term equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Unemployment rate is currently low at 4.5%.
failure_condition: Average 20-day forward returns when UNRATE < 4.7% are not statistically significantly greater than when UNRATE >= 4.7%.

mechanism_narrative: Strong momentum in retail sales (RSAFS) reflects robust consumer health, which translates directly into higher corporate revenues and positive investor sentiment. This effect is most pronounced over short horizons.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending is approximately 70% of GDP.
failure_condition: A strategy going long S&P 500 when the 3-month change in RSAFS is positive and short when negative fails to generate a positive Sharpe ratio over a 5-year backtest.

mechanism_narrative: When the real Federal Funds Rate (approximated by FEDFUNDS minus CPIAUCSL year-over-year percent change) is high and positive, monetary policy is actively restrictive, contracting credit and liquidity, which negatively impacts equity valuations.
evidence_refs: fact:The current Fed Funds rate of 5.25% is well above the recent inflation rate.
failure_condition: The average 60-day forward return is not negative in more than 50% of the months where the trigger condition is met.

mechanism_narrative: A significant deceleration in the quarter-over-quarter growth rate of real GDP (GDPC1) signals a potential inflection point in the business cycle, leading investors to de-risk portfolios and causing negative forward returns.
evidence_refs: fact:The most recent GDP print for Q1 2007 was weak, showing signs of slowing.
failure_condition: In the 60 trading days following a GDP report that meets the trigger condition, the S&P 500 posts a positive return more than 50% of the time.
